name: Czechia
summary: Czechia is a real study->Employee-Card->PR route for two engineers, with a booming Prague/Brno tech market, low ~15% flat tax and dual citizenship allowed as the biggest levers; the biggest drag is access - no Dhaka mission (all long-stay visas decided in New Delhi under a hard 50-application quota), a ~10-year B1-Czech citizenship clock, only a 9-month post-study job-search window, a thin Bangladeshi/Muslim community and a new Babis anti-migration coalition.

links.title: Study in Czechia (official national portal)
Immigration Portal of the Czech Republic (ipc.gov.cz, official)

job-market.summary: Lowest-unemployment EU economy with deep IT shortage; mid SW comp ~CZK 1.1-1.5M, AI/ML CZK 1.1-1.8M, both engineers absorbable.
job-market.reasoning: Czechia has the EU's lowest unemployment and a structural IT/engineering shortage, so software, data and AI/ML engineers are among the most sought-after roles and visa sponsorship runs through government Highly-Qualified Worker / Blue Card schemes. Mid-career software comp sits around CZK 1.1-1.5M with seniors at CZK 1.7-2.7M+, and AI/ML engineers command CZK 1.1-1.8M, with Red Hat, Oracle, IBM, Kiwi.com and dozens of multinational hubs hiring in English in Prague and Brno. Both spouses are readily employable; held just below top-tier because the absolute market is smaller than Germany/UK and some local roles still expect Czech.
evidence: EU's lowest unemployment + structural IT/engineering shortage (2025-2026)
Mid SW comp ~CZK 1.1-1.5M; senior CZK 1.7-2.7M+ (Glassdoor/Levels.fyi, 2026)
AI/ML engineer CZK 1.1-1.8M, GenAI/AI engineer CZK 1.0-1.7M (Nucamp 2026)
Govt Highly-Qualified Worker & Blue Card schemes prioritise skilled non-EU
English-speaking employers: Red Hat, Oracle, IBM, Kiwi.com, Productboard

links.title: AI Salaries in the Czech Republic in 2026 (Nucamp)
Software Engineer Salary in Czech Republic (Levels.fyi)

visa-access.summary: No Dhaka mission; all long-stay visas decided in New Delhi under a hard 50-application quota with BD-specific 1-2 month notice delays.
visa-access.reasoning: There is no Czech mission in Dhaka, so Bangladeshi nationals must apply for long-stay study/work visas at the Czech Embassy in New Delhi, which caps the Bhutan/Maldives/Sri Lanka/Bangladesh/Nepal long-term intake at 50 applications per registration window (>=20 reserved for family reunification, <=5 seasonal). Bangladeshi appointments are deliberately assigned with 1-2 months' advance notice 'due to the current situation' in Bangladesh, applicants must show 2 years' continuous legal residence if applying as non-jurisdiction nationals, and the email-registration system disqualifies any double submission. Blue Card has a separate larger queue (~250-300), but the New-Delhi routing, tight study/work quota and BD-specific friction place this near the severe-barrier band.
evidence: No Czech mission in Dhaka; long-stay visas decided in New Delhi (official)
Quota 50 applications/window; >=20 family reunion, <=5 seasonal (official)
2026 registration windows: May 4, Jun 1, Jul 7, Aug 3, Sep 7 (New Delhi)
BD appointments given 1-2 months' notice 'due to current situation'
Separate Blue Card queue ~250-300 applications (visasupdate, 2026)
Processing ~7-30 days once submitted; one email per applicant or disqualified

links.title: Long-term Visa Appointments for Bangladesh etc. (Czech Embassy New Delhi, official)
Czech Embassy New Delhi Appointment System 2026 (visasupdate)

citizenship.summary: Dual citizenship allowed since 2014 (no renunciation), but ~10yr legal-residence clock + B1 Czech exam + civics/integration test.
citizenship.reasoning: Czech law has permitted multiple citizenship since 1 January 2014 with no renunciation required, and Bangladesh allows dual nationality with EU states, so this couple could naturalise while keeping Bangladeshi citizenship - a genuine plus given their dual-passport preference. But the clock is long: naturalisation needs 5 years of permanent residence (itself reached after ~5 years of temporary residence) or effectively 10 years' total legal residence, plus an ALTE-certified B1 Czech exam, a constitutional/history civics test and proven integration and self-sufficiency. The ~10-year effective path and B1 Czech bar hold it at the mixed band despite the dual-citizenship advantage.
evidence: Multiple citizenship allowed since 01.01.2014; no renunciation required
Bangladesh permits dual nationality with EU countries
Naturalisation: 5yr permanent residence OR ~10yr total legal residence
B1 Czech exam (ALTE-certified) + constitutional/history civics test
Must prove integration (family/work/social) and self-sufficiency

post-study-work.summary: 9-month job-seeker residence (free labour-market access) after a Czech degree, then in-country switch to Employee Card/Blue Card; spouse can work.
post-study-work.reasoning: Graduates of an accredited Czech university programme can take a long-term residence permit for seeking employment or starting a business for up to 9 months, during which they have free, unrestricted access to the labour market, then switch in-country to an Employee Card (up to 2 years, renewable) or an EU Blue Card. A Czech degree also grants free labour-market access on the Employee Card without a separate work permit, which helps both spouses. The 9-month window is non-extendable and meaningfully shorter than Germany's 18 months or multi-year Australia/Canada post-study windows, so it sits in the lower-middle band; the 2026 90-day Employee Card decision guarantee helps.
evidence: 9-month job-seeker / business residence after a Czech degree (ipc.gov.cz)
Free, unrestricted labour-market access during the job-seeker permit
Switch in-country to Employee Card (up to 2 yrs) or EU Blue Card
Czech-degree Employee Card needs no separate work permit
Window non-extendable; shorter than DE 18mo / AU-CA multi-year PSW

Getting a work visa after studies (Study in Czechia, official)

spouse-family.summary: Both can hold own permits and work; spouse of an Employee/Blue Card holder can work via reunification, but study-phase dependent work isn't automatic.
spouse-family.reasoning: As two qualified engineers the strongest path is for each to hold an independent student then Employee Card/Blue Card permit, so both work freely in the work phase. A spouse joining a Blue Card or Employee Card holder via family reunification (after the sponsor's ~6 months' residence for Employee Card) can generally work without a separate permit, and reunited family children access free public schooling. The weaker point is the study phase: a 2023 amendment removed the adult-dependant family-reunion-via-study route, so during the MSc the partner should ideally enrol or hold their own permit rather than rely on a dependant visa. Strong overall but not the seamless both-work-in-every-phase top anchor.
evidence: Each partner can hold own student/Employee Card/Blue Card permit
Blue Card/Employee Card reunification lets spouse work without extra permit
Employee Card sponsor needs ~6 months' residence to reunite family
2023 amendment removed adult-dependant family reunion via study
Reunited children have access to free public schooling

msc-study.summary: English-taught CS MSc widely available at ~CZK 100k-400k/yr (~EUR 4k-16k); Czech-taught is free; students work freely, spouse can join.
msc-study.reasoning: Charles University, CTU Prague and Masaryk (Brno) offer English-taught CS Master's, with public-university English tuition typically CZK 100,000-400,000/year (~EUR 4,000-16,000); Czech-language programmes at public universities are tuition-free for all nationalities but Czech is hard to reach at entry. Students may work without a separate permit and a spouse can accompany, and the low cost of living eases the total study budget. Solidly good given the strong tech ecosystem, but not the near-free, abundant-English profile of Germany.
evidence: English-taught Master's tuition ~CZK 100k-400k/yr (~EUR 4k-16k)
Czech-language programmes tuition-free for all nationalities (public unis)
Charles University, CTU Prague, Masaryk offer English CS Master's
Students work without a separate work permit; spouse may accompany
Lower living costs than Western Europe ease total study budget

pr-pathway.summary: PR after 5 years' continuous residence, but student time counts only half; A2 Czech exam (new format Apr 2026) and clean record required.
pr-pathway.reasoning: Third-country nationals can obtain permanent residence after 5 years of continuous temporary residence, a clear standard route. The catch for this couple is that time on a student residence permit counts only at half value, so a 2-year MSc adds only 1 year toward PR - they would need ~4 more years on an Employee Card/Blue Card to reach 5, making the realistic PR horizon ~6 years from arrival. An A2 Czech exam (held in a new format since 11 April 2026) and a clean financial/criminal record are also required. Standard-paced and predictable, hence the middle band.
evidence: PR after 5 years' continuous residence (ipc.gov.cz)
Student-permit time counts at HALF toward the 5 years
MSc 2 yrs = ~1 yr credit; then ~4 yrs Employee/Blue Card needed
A2 Czech exam required; new exam format since 11.04.2026
Clean financial and criminal record required

income-cost.summary: Prague SW comp ~CZK 1.1-1.7M; single-person ~CZK 20k/mo excl. rent, 1-bed centre ~CZK 25.7k - two low-taxed incomes save well.
income-cost.reasoning: Mid-career software comp in Prague runs roughly CZK 1.1-1.5M (seniors CZK 1.7-2.7M+), while Numbeo (Jun 2026) puts a single person's monthly cost at ~CZK 20,000 (~EUR 845) excluding rent and a city-centre 1-bed at ~CZK 25,700 (~EUR 1,086), with Brno noticeably cheaper. Gross pay is below Western Europe, but a low ~15% income tax and ~11.6% employee contributions plus cheaper housing mean two engineering incomes save comfortably. Net savings potential is good rather than exceptional given lower headline salaries and rising Prague rents.
evidence: Prague mid SW comp ~CZK 1.1-1.5M; senior CZK 1.7-2.7M+ (2026)
Single-person monthly cost ~CZK 20,000 (~EUR 845) excl. rent (Numbeo Jun 2026)
Prague 1-bed city centre ~CZK 25,700 (~EUR 1,086); outside ~CZK 22,000
Brno ~CZK 93k buys Prague-CZK 110k lifestyle (Numbeo comparison)
Low ~15% tax + ~11.6% contributions preserve take-home for two earners

healthcare.summary: Universal public insurance (13.5% payroll) for employees - good quality; non-EU students must buy commercial cover ~CZK 7k-13k/yr.
healthcare.reasoning: Czechia runs universal public health insurance funded by a 13.5%-of-salary payroll contribution (employee 4.5%), giving employed residents comprehensive, good-quality access to GPs, specialists and hospitals at modest cost; VZP is the largest insurer (~60% of the population). The gap for this couple is the study phase: non-EU students who don't work cannot join the public system and must buy commercial foreigners' insurance, typically CZK 7,000-13,000/year (~EUR 280-520), and health cover is mandatory for any stay over 90 days. Quality and cost are solid once both are working, so it sits in the decent-with-newcomer-gaps band.
evidence: Universal public insurance funded by 13.5% payroll (employee 4.5%)
VZP is the largest public insurer (~60% of the population)
Non-EU non-working students must buy commercial insurance
Commercial student cover ~CZK 7,000-13,000/yr (~EUR 280-520)
Health insurance mandatory for stays over 90 days / residence permits

culture-language.summary: EF EPI 2025 rank #23 (B2, score 582); English works in Prague/Brno tech, but Czech needed for officialdom, A2 for PR, B1 for citizenship.
culture-language.reasoning: Czechia ranks #23 globally on the EF English Proficiency Index 2025 (score 582, 'upper-intermediate' B2), with Brno (620) outscoring Prague (576), so English is workable in tech workplaces and among younger Czechs, and livability is high with low cost and central-Europe access. However Czech is a hard Slavic language needed for bureaucracy and life outside the hubs, with an A2 exam for permanent residence and a B1 exam plus civics test for citizenship, and EF notes progress has stalled for five years. This local-language bar for officialdom and the couple's passport goal keeps it in the mixed band.
evidence: EF EPI 2025: rank #23 globally, score 582, B2 'upper-intermediate'
Brno 620 vs Prague 576; speaking weakest skill (498)
Czech required for officialdom and life outside major cities
A2 Czech exam for permanent residence; B1 for citizenship
High livability, low cost of living, central Schengen location

safety-law.summary: WJP Rule of Law Index 2025: rank 20 of 140; Prague crime index ~24 (vs Berlin 45, Paris 58); one of Europe's safest.
safety-law.reasoning: Czechia ranks 20th of 140 on the WJP Rule of Law Index 2025 (15th of 31 in the EU/EFTA/North America region), confirming sound, broadly independent courts. Violent crime is low - Prague's Numbeo crime index is ~24, far below Berlin (~45) or Paris (~58) - and US/UK/Australian advisories all sit at 'exercise normal precautions' in early 2026. Held below the Nordics/Germany because the migrant experience is weaker amid a hardening anti-immigrant political climate, but a strong factor overall for this couple.
evidence: WJP Rule of Law Index 2025: rank 20 of 140 (country profile)
15th of 31 in EU/EFTA/North America region
Prague Numbeo crime index ~24 (vs Berlin ~45, Paris ~58)

politics.summary: Babis ANO+SPD+Motorists coalition (108/200) won confidence Jan 2026, survived no-confidence Feb 2026; 'zero-tolerance' migration, rejects EU Pact.
politics.reasoning: Andrej Babis was sworn in as PM in December 2025 leading an ANO + anti-migrant SPD + Motorists coalition holding 108 of 200 seats; it won a confidence vote 108-91 on 13-15 January 2026 and survived a no-confidence motion in February 2026, so it is stable in the near term. The platform is 'zero-tolerance' for illegal migration, a new Migration Act and rejection of the EU Migration Pact, with stricter foreigner/asylum rules phasing in through June 2026. Babis's prior government did expand fast-track IT visa schemes, so skilled-tech routes are likely preserved by economic pragmatism, but the hardening anti-immigrant tone weighs the score into the weak band for a non-EU Muslim couple.
evidence: Babis sworn in PM Dec 2025; ANO+SPD+Motorists, 108/200 seats
Won confidence vote 108-91 (13-15 Jan 2026)
Survived no-confidence motion Feb 2026 (coalition durable near-term)
Platform: zero-tolerance migration, new Migration Act, rejects EU Pact

tax.summary: Low flat-ish tax: 15% up to ~CZK 1.76M, 23% above; employee contributions ~11.6% (7.1% social + 4.5% health); strong take-home.
tax.reasoning: Czechia taxes personal income at 15% up to CZK 1,762,812 (~EUR 71,000) in 2026 and 23% only above that, with employee social and health contributions of ~11.6% (7.1% social security + 4.5% health) and a basic taxpayer credit of CZK 30,840. For typical software salaries the all-in effective burden is roughly 25-34%, clearly lighter than Germany's 35-40%, giving these engineers a strong take-home. Among the better tax environments in this set; held below 80 because the 23% band and uncapped health levy bite at senior-engineer pay.
evidence: Income tax 15% up to CZK 1,762,812 (~EUR 71k), 23% above (2026)
Employee contributions ~11.6% (7.1% social + 4.5% health) in 2026
Social-security cap CZK 2,350,416; no cap on health contributions
Basic taxpayer credit CZK 30,840 lowers effective rate at mid pay
Effective all-in burden ~25-34% for SW salaries vs DE ~35-40%

muslim-diaspora.summary: Very small Muslim community (~20k, ~0.2%); ~2 Prague mosques + basement prayer rooms, limited halal, documented Islamophobia.
muslim-diaspora.reasoning: Czechia has only ~20,000 Muslims (~0.2% of the population), no large purpose-built mosques (Prague has ~2 plus small/basement prayer rooms), and halal food is hard to find outside Prague. The Bangladeshi/South Asian community is negligible - the largest Asian groups are Vietnamese (~63,000), Mongolian and Chinese, with no notable Bangladeshi presence - and Islamophobia is well documented, with anti-Islam rhetoric normalised in mainstream politics since 2014-2015 and reinforced by the current SPD-backed coalition. This is a weak environment for a Muslim Bangladeshi couple seeking community.
evidence: ~20,000 Muslims (~0.2% of population)
Prague has ~2 mosques plus small/basement prayer rooms; none large purpose-built
Halal food hard to find outside Prague
Largest Asian groups Vietnamese ~63k, Mongolian, Chinese; BD negligible
Documented Islamophobia; anti-Islam rhetoric normalised since 2014-2015
